meta.title: Color Blindness
meta.author: Cheryl Reifsnyder
meta.focusQuestion: What causes color blindness, and how can it affect a person's life?

sections.title: What Colors Do You See?
paragraphs: Imagine looking at a box of crayons and seeing only shades of gray, blue, and yellow, or looking at a traffic signal on which the red and green lights look very similar. That's how the world can appear to someone who is color-blind. As you can imagine, this can cause problems!

Color blindness is the world's most common genetic disorder, affecting more than ten million people in the United States alone. Despite its name, though, color blindness does not cause blindness. Most people affected by color blindness can still see some colors. Color blindness simply means the inability or decreased ability to see certain colors.

sections.title: Where Do Colors Come From?
paragraphs: It's easier to understand color blindness when you realize that colors are actually different wavelengths of light. If you use a prism to separate white light into a rainbow, you're actually separating it into its different wavelengths. The red end of the spectrum contains longer wavelengths of light while shorter wavelengths of light are at the purple end.

Objects appear to be different colors because they absorb and reflect different wavelengths of light. A strawberry looks red because it reflects long (red) wavelengths. If it instead reflected short wavelengths of light, it would look blue!

sections.title: How Color Vision Works
paragraphs: Humans see colors because light-detecting cells in our eyes respond differently to different wavelengths of light.

When light enters one of your eyes, the lens focuses it on the retina, a thin layer of tissue at the back of the eyeball. There it activates light-sensitive cells, called rod cells and cone cells. Activated cells send electrical signals to the brain, where they are translated into images.

sections.title: Rods and Cones
paragraphs: Rod cells are the most sensitive cells in your retina. They allow you to see in extremely dim light, but they can't tell different colors apart. Cone cells are responsible for color vision. Each cell contains one of three different light-absorbing molecules, called photopigments, found which detect blue, green, or red light. Combining these three main colors allows you to see thousands of colors. When one or more photopigments aren't functioning properly, though, color blindness results.

sections.title: Different Types of Color Blindness
paragraphs: Red-green color blindness, which causes difficulty when it comes to telling the difference between red and green, is the most common type of color vision problem. It's hereditary, found most often in people of northern European ancestry. It's also more common in men, of which it affects one out of twelve, than in women, of which it affects only one in two hundred.

Red-green color blindness is usually caused by defects in the green photopigment. Without the green photopigment, green and yellow objects appear reddish in color. Defects in the red photopigment also cause red-green color blindness. In this case, red, orange, and yellow objects appear green or black.

Blue-yellow color blindness occurs when the blue photopigment is defective or missing. It's fairly rare, only occurring in about one out of every ten thousand people worldwide. Unlike red-green color blindness, it's equally common in men and women.

Complete color blindness is the most severe form of color blindness. It occurs when there are defects in two or more of the three types of cones. People who are missing two or more types of cones tend to be extra-sensitive to light. They may also have difficulty seeing clearly, especially in bright light.
Only about one out of thirty thousand people worldwide are missing all three types of cones. People who lack any functioning cone cells see the world in shades of black, white, and gray.

sections.title: Color Blindness Is No Joke
paragraphs: Color blindness can cause serious problems. People who are color-blind have trouble reading color-coded information on maps, graphs, and charts. Children with color blindness may have trouble in school, especially if their color blindness has not been diagnosed. Red-green color blindness makes it difficult to read yellow chalk on a green chalkboard.

Color blindness can also cause safety issues. Fire hydrants, car warning lights, and emergency equipment are often colored red or yellow to make them more visible. These bright colors may be obvious to those with full-color vision but unnoticeable to those who are color-blind.

Color blindness can even limit a person's career options. Some positions, such as geologist or commercial airline pilot, require full-color vision. Other careers, such as interior design, photography, and food inspection, are significantly more difficult with color vision defects.

sections.title: Testing for Color Blindness
paragraphs: The Ishihara Test is the most common test for red-green color vision problems. It uses images made up of dots that are different sizes and colors. People with full-color vision can see numbers or shapes inside the pictures. People with red-green color blindness find it difficult or impossible to identify the hidden symbols.
Other, similar tests are used to detect yellow-blue color blindness.

sections.title: Tools to Help Color-Blind People
paragraphs: Currently, there's no known cure for color blindness. However, modern technology has led to the development of many new tools to help people who are color-blind. Smartphone apps perform tasks such as identifying colors and recommending complementary colors.

Some smartphone apps can only identify colors after the user snaps a photo, but others can analyze them in the camera's viewfinder without requiring the user to take a photo first. This lets users identify colors in real time.

Researchers have also created high-tech tinted lenses that filter out specific wavelengths of light. Special sunglasses that use these lenses allow people with red-green color blindness to distinguish between the two colors.

sections.title: Treating Color Blindness
paragraphs: Although no true treatment for color blindness currently exists, that may soon change. In recent experiments, scientists were able to correct red-green color blindness in squirrel monkeys. The monkeys lacked the red photopigment and, as a result, couldn't tell the difference between red and green. Researchers engineered a specially constructed virus to insert the red photopigment gene into the monkeys' green cone cells. Within about twenty weeks, treated monkeys could distinguish between red and green dots.

This was exciting news! The monkeys had been color-blind since birth. Many doctors and scientists had predicted that it would be impossible to correct their color blindness because the monkeys' brains would not have developed the connections needed to interpret color information.
Now that we know that color blindness can be cured in adult squirrel monkeys, there's hope for a cure in adult humans, too.

sections.title: Color Vision for All?
paragraphs: Every year, researchers gain a better understanding of color blindness and its causes. New tools help color-blind people choose ripe fruit and pick out matching clothes. People with red-green color blindness can use special glasses to allow them to tell the difference between shades of red and green.
Best of all, a treatment for color blindness may be available in the near future. Maybe someday soon, color blindness will become a thing of the past!

vocabulary.sentence: It's hereditary, found most often in people of northern European ancestry.

vocabulary.definition: cells in the eye's retina that are color sensitive and work best in bright light

options: cells that see in dim light
cells in the retina that are color sensitive and work best in bright light
cells that send sound to the brain

vocabulary.sentence: <s>Red-green color blindness</s> <v>is usually caused by</v> <o>defects in the green photopigment</o>.

vocabulary.sentence: Color blindness is the world's most common genetic disorder.

options: a condition caused by eating bad food
an inherited condition caused by a problem in one or more genes
a condition caused by bright lights

options: of or relating to genetic traits passed from parents to offspring

the colored part of the eye

vocabulary.sentence: Rod cells are the most sensitive cells in your retina. They allow you to see in extremely dim light, but they can't tell different colors apart.

questions.question: Natural materials that change when they absorb light are called ___.
questions.evidence: Each cell contains one of three different light-absorbing molecules, called photopigments.
choices: molecules
photopigments
cells
prisms
questions.explanation: Photopigments are natural compounds that undergo chemical changes when they absorb light.

questions.question: What is the author's purpose for writing Color Blindness?
questions.evidence: Color blindness is the world's most common genetic disorder, affecting more than ten million people in the United States alone.
choices: to convince readers to get their vision checked and be sure they are not color blind
to inform readers about the effectiveness of color-blindness testing
to inform readers about the causes of color blindness and how it affects people
to inform readers about the differences between how humans and other animals see
questions.explanation: The author's purpose is to inform readers about what causes color blindness and how it affects people's lives.

questions.question: One way doctors can test for color blindness is ___.
questions.evidence: The Ishihara Test is the most common test for red-green color vision problems. It uses images made up of dots that are different sizes and colors.
choices: by using a smartphone app
by having someone pick out an outfit to see if the clothes match
by using different colored flashing lights
by using images made up of dots that are different sizes and colors
questions.explanation: The Ishihara Test uses images made up of dots of different sizes and colors to test for color blindness.

questions.question: What can you conclude about the studies done on squirrel monkeys?
questions.evidence: In recent experiments, scientists were able to correct red-green color blindness in squirrel monkeys...Now that we know that color blindness can be cured in adult squirrel monkeys, there's hope for a cure in adult humans, too.
choices: The experiments show that color blindness can be cured in monkeys but cannot be cured in humans.
The experiments show that there is no cure for color blindness.
The experiments give hope for curing color blindness in humans.
The experiments show that squirrel monkeys can help people who are color-blind.
questions.explanation: Scientists cured color blindness in monkeys, giving hope that similar treatments could work in humans.

questions.question: The cells in the eyes that are light-sensitive and work best in dim light are called ___.
questions.evidence: Rod cells are the most sensitive cells in your retina. They allow you to see in extremely dim light, but they can't tell different colors apart.
choices: rod cells
retina cells
tissue cells
cone cells
questions.explanation: Rod cells are light-sensitive cells that work best in dim light but can't detect colors.

questions.question: What is the author's purpose for writing the section 'Tools to Help Color-Blind People'?
questions.evidence: Currently, there's no known cure for color blindness. However, modern technology has led to the development of many new tools to help people who are color-blind.
choices: to inform the reader about advances in technology that support people with color blindness
to persuade the reader that smartphone apps are more effective in helping people with color blindness than sunglasses with special lenses
to inform the reader about what life was like for color-blind people before there were smartphone apps
to persuade the reader that special glasses are the best way for people with red-green color blindness to see a more colorful world
questions.explanation: The author's purpose is to inform readers about technological advances (apps, special lenses) that help color-blind people.

questions.question: Red-green color blindness causes people ___.
questions.evidence: Defects in the red photopigment also cause red-green color blindness. In this case, red, orange, and yellow objects appear green or black.
choices: to be overly sensitive to bright lights
to see everything in shades of black, white, and gray
to see red and green as gray
to see red, orange, and yellow objects as green or black
questions.explanation: Red-green color blindness can cause red, orange, and yellow objects to appear green or black.

questions.question: What is the last step in the process of an eye seeing?
questions.evidence: Activated cells send electrical signals to the brain, where they are translated into images.
choices: Activated cells send electrical signals to the brain.
Light enters one of the eyes.
The iris opens or closes the pupil to control light entering.
The cornea gathers and begins to focus light.
questions.explanation: The last step is when activated rod and cone cells send electrical signals to the brain, which translates them into images.

questions.question: Which of the following is an opinion?
questions.evidence: Color blindness can cause serious problems.
choices: Doctors use several types of tests to detect color blindness.
Color blindness is fascinating.
Most animals can see fewer colors than humans.
Children with color blindness may have trouble in school.
questions.explanation: 'Color blindness is fascinating' is an opinion because it expresses a personal feeling, not a fact that can be proven.

questions.question: A physical condition that is caused by a problem in one or more genes is called ___.
questions.evidence: Color blindness is the world's most common genetic disorder.
choices: a defect
hereditary
a genetic disorder
research
questions.explanation: A genetic disorder is an inherited physical condition caused by a problem in one or more genes.

questions.question: What kind of tools are available to help people deal with color blindness?
questions.evidence: Smartphone apps perform tasks such as identifying colors and recommending complementary colors. Researchers have also created high-tech tinted lenses that filter out specific wavelengths of light.
questions.explanation: Smartphone apps can identify colors and recommend complementary colors. Special sunglasses with tinted lenses can help distinguish colors.
opinionPrompts: How do apps help color-blind people?

questions.question: In what ways can color blindness cause safety issues?
questions.evidence: Fire hydrants, car warning lights, and emergency equipment are often colored red or yellow to make them more visible. These bright colors may be obvious to those with full-color vision but unnoticeable to those who are color-blind.
questions.explanation: Fire hydrants, car warning lights, and emergency equipment are often red or yellow — colors that color-blind people may not notice.
opinionPrompts: Why are warning lights colored red or yellow?

authorPurpose.prompt: What was the author's purpose for writing Color Blindness? Check all that apply and find evidence from the text.

evidencePrompts.Inform: Write details showing the author is giving information:
evidencePrompts.Entertain: Write details showing the author is trying to entertain:
evidencePrompts.Persuade: Write details showing the author is trying to persuade:

answerKey.Inform: The book gives detailed facts about color blindness: causes, types, effects on daily life, testing methods, and research. Example: 'Color blindness is the world's most common genetic disorder, affecting more than ten million people in the United States alone.'
answerKey.Entertain: The author uses engaging language and scenarios to keep readers interested. Example: 'Imagine looking at a box of crayons and seeing only shades of gray, blue, and yellow.' The stories about squirrel monkeys and future cures are also entertaining.
answerKey.Persuade: The author does NOT appear to be trying to persuade readers of anything. The text presents facts without trying to change opinions or behavior.

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Cone cells and rod cells work together to help us see, but when photopigments malfunction, color blindness results.
keyDetails: Rod cells are the most sensitive cells — they let you see in dim light but can't tell colors apart
Cone cells are responsible for color vision, each containing one of three photopigments
The three photopigments detect blue, green, or red light
When one or more photopigments aren't functioning properly, color blindness results
distractors: Rod cells detect red and green light
Cone cells only work in complete darkness

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Color blindness can cause problems in school, create safety risks, and limit career options.
keyDetails: Color-blind people have trouble reading color-coded information on maps and charts
Safety issues arise because warning lights and fire hydrants may be unnoticeable
Some careers like pilot and geologist require full-color vision
Children may struggle in school if their condition hasn't been diagnosed
distractors: Color blindness improves your night vision
All careers are equally easy for color-blind people

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Although no cure exists yet, gene therapy experiments on monkeys give hope for future human treatments.
keyDetails: Scientists corrected red-green color blindness in squirrel monkeys
They used a virus to insert the red photopigment gene into cone cells
Within twenty weeks, treated monkeys could distinguish red from green
This was surprising because doctors thought adult brains couldn't develop color connections
distractors: A cure is already available for humans
The monkeys were born with normal color vision

intro.title: Genetic Disorder
intro.content: An inherited condition caused by a problem in your genes. Color blindness is passed down from parents!

intro.title: Summarize
intro.content: A summary tells the MOST important details: Who? What? When? Where? Why? Only the key facts!

wavelengths.title: Wavelengths
wavelengths.content: The distance between the high points of a light wave. Different wavelengths = different colors!

wavelengths.title: Prism
wavelengths.content: A clear object that bends light to separate it into rainbow colors. Like a crystal!

rodscones.title: Photopigments
rodscones.content: Special molecules in cone cells that absorb light and detect blue, green, or red colors.

rodscones.title: Hyphenated Compound Adjectives
rodscones.content: 'light-absorbing molecules' — 'light-absorbing' is TWO words joined with a hyphen to describe 'molecules'. These are hyphenated compound adjectives!

types.title: Hereditary
types.content: Passed down from parents to children through genes. Red-green color blindness is hereditary.

types.title: Ancestry
types.content: Your family history — where your family came from generations ago.

types.title: Author's Purpose
types.content: Is the author trying to INFORM you (give facts), ENTERTAIN you (tell fun stories), or PERSUADE you (change your mind)? Look for clues!

effects.title: Hyphenated Compound Adjectives
effects.content: 'color-coded information' — 'color-coded' is two words joined with a hyphen to describe 'information'. Find more on pages 8-11!

tools.title: Complementary
tools.content: Serving to complete or balance something. Complementary colors go well together — like blue and orange!
